title: Chapter 16: Ash and Mint Tobacco
paragraphs: Rumors in a fading world do not spread like fire, but like a thick, low-creeping mist. They seep into the cracks of taverns, settle on the armor of weary guards, and are absorbed into the gray dust of the Great Tract.

Taeron, the red-haired elf, kept his word. Reaching the nearest outpost, he did not weave tall tales of a heroic battle against overwhelming forces. He told them honestly, looking his commanders straight in the eye, how his life was bought by a tiny goblin with a smoldering stick. Some called it the feverish delirium of a wounded man. Others, a cunning illusion of demons. But in Taeron's eyes remained a quiet, almost painful realization: all their long lives, his people had despised the wrong beings.

Yashil knew nothing of this. Wordly fame held no weight for him. Two years had passed since he left the ashes of the Rotten Hollow. These years had not gone unnoticed: Yashil had grown, his shoulders had strengthened from hard work, and his gaze had lost its childhood naivety, gaining the depth that frightened some and gave hope to others. He sat for a long time on the edge of the ravine, listening to the rustle of dead grass and waiting for night meetings with Kirah.

The werewolf Dzhanavar, to Yashil's surprise, also did not dissolve into the wastelands. The huge wolf made his lair in the ruins of an old windmill, whose millstones had long since overgrown with poisonous-blue moss, and appeared exactly when the silence became too heavy.

"A true lone wolf does not attach himself to a place, Green," Dzhanavar declared one evening, stretching out on the broken roof of the mill. "I'm just... strategically anchored on the height. For perimeter control. Tactics, brother. Auf."
"You just don't want to fall asleep alone in the dark," Yashil responded calmly, not taking his eyes off the horizon.
"A low blow," the werewolf winced. "You hit harder with words than those bandits did with steel."
She appeared in the third week after the blood had dried on the white stones of the Tract.

Yashil was sitting in his usual spot when the wind suddenly changed. Instead of the usual smell of ozone, rot, and burnt copper, the air brought something completely alien. The sweet aroma of old, faded perfume, the tart smell of smoldering mint tobacco and pine needles.
The goblin slowly turned around.

On a fallen, withered tree trunk behind him sat a being seemingly woven from the contradictions of a bygone era. A graceful humanoid body was encased in a worn leather corset and torn stockings, over which a heavy elven-work cloak hung carelessly. Her hands were hidden in long fingerless gloves, clinking quietly with copper bracelets.

But the head—it was a fox muzzle. Elongated, red, with sharp ears and clever, yellow, topaz-like eyes. These eyes looked at the world with such deep, completely sincere exhaustion that one look made you want to apologize for the very fact of your existence.

The fox held a long bone mouthpiece in her thin clawed fingers, from which a trickle of caustic, greenish smoke curled. Her dark fox lips were touched with cherry paint. She took a deep draw, released a ring of smoke into the ashen sky, and spoke in a voice like silk soaked in poison and old wine:
"So, you are that very idiot."
Yashil blinked, not moving.

"The very goblin who threw himself at thugs to save an elf whose people have for millennia considered your race a mistake of nature," she continued, crossing her legs. "It's impressive. Infinitely stupid, but impressive. I am Rene."

She held out a gloved hand to him. The gesture was slow, as if she were giving him time to change his mind. Yashil, looking at the graceful claws, calmly shook her hand. Rene's fingers proved unexpectedly strong and cold.
"Why have you come, Rene?" Yashil asked quietly.
The fox drew again, narrowing her yellow eyes.

"Boredom, my dear green friend. A monstrous, all-consuming boredom from which the soul rots," she brushed ash onto the dead earth. "I've traveled half of this doomed continent. I've cleaned out the treasuries of three lords, slept in silks, broken a dozen hearts in high towers, and I still have nothing to occupy myself with. The world is dying so slowly and ungracefully. And here—a goblin who intends to face this decay with an open heart. This," she pointed her mouthpiece straight at him, "is the most absurd and intriguing thing I've encountered in the last half-century."
"I'm not trying to save the world," Yashil shook his head. "That is impossible. I'm just trying not to become part of it."

"Oh. That is even more meaningless, and therefore—even more beautiful," the fox smiled weakly. In this smile there was no warmth, only bitter irony. "Perhaps I'll linger. I'll watch from the front row as this cruel universe breaks you. Or... as you break it."

Kirah came at night. He paced with heavy paws on the withered grass, trying not to make noise. Seeing Rene sitting by a tiny campfire, the demon froze. Two glowing red eyes widened. Claws ground out of his fingers, veins on his resinous body pulsed threateningly.
"This... what is it?" Kirah rasped, instinctively shielding Yashil.
"This is Rene," the goblin answered calmly, throwing a dry branch onto the fire. "She is... a friend. Probably."
"I am an observer," Rene corrected lazily, not looking up from her cigarette. "The word 'friend' implies obligations. And obligations are exhausting."

Kirah froze, towering over her like a black mountain. He was created to tear flesh and ruin walls, but before the naked, bored cynicism of this fox, he proved absolutely defenseless. He didn't know how to react to someone who wasn't afraid of him.
Rene measured the giant with a long, evaluating look.

"A demon of Zahan who is afraid to hurt anyone," she stated, releasing a stream of mint smoke. Then she turned her gaze to Yashil. "A goblin who wants to save everyone. And a fox who pretends she doesn't care. We all lie to ourselves, Horned. The difference is that I have enough style to do it gracefully."

Kirah opened his fang-filled maw, trying to find a worthy response, but the words stuck in his throat. He sank down by the fire like a huge, clumsy shadow, trying to tuck in his tail so as not to take up too much space.

The silence was broken by the crunch of branches. Out of the impenetrable darkness into the campfire's light came Dzhanavar, dragging behind him the heavy carcass of a cave boar. The werewolf dropped the catch by the fire, eyed the assembled company, and bared his teeth in a broad grin.

"Now this I understand," the wolf drawled in a velvet bass, driving his axe into the ground. "Do you know what they said in my forest? It doesn't matter who stands against you. It matters who you share a piece of meat with by the night fire. Auf, gentlemen. I like it here."
So around one dim, struggling-with-the-cold-wind campfire, a company gathered that no chronicler would dare describe.
Goblin, Demon, Werewolf, and Fox.

Four absolute outcasts. Each of them was crossed out, rejected, and cursed by the unwritten laws of this world. But here, listening to the crackle of burning branches and the howl of the icy wasteland wind, they paradoxically complemented each other.

Yashil looked at the sparks flying into the black sky. For the first time in his short life, he realized a simple truth. Perhaps, to withstand the end of the world, an army of steel-clad righteous men or ancient magic is not needed. Perhaps four broken beings who had the courage to stay together are enough.
